issue_title: JSON Leftovers
issue_body:
The socket read is run through the JSON

>     def refresh(self, gpsd_data_package):
        """Sets new socket data as Fix attributes
        Arguments:
            self:
            gpsd_data_package (json object):
        Provides:
        self attribute dictionaries, e.g., self.TPV['lat'], self.SKY['gdop']
        Raises:
        AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'keys' when the device falls out of the system
        ValueError, KeyError: most likely extra, or mangled JSON data, should not happen, but that
        applies to a lot of things.
        """
        try:
            fresh_data = json.loads(gpsd_data_package)  # The reserved word 'class' is popped from JSON object class
            package_name = fresh_data.pop('class', 'ERROR')  # gpsd data package errors are also 'ERROR'.
            package = getattr(self, package_name, package_name)  # packages are named for JSON object class
            for key in package.keys():  # TODO: Rollover and retry.  It fails here when device disappears
                package[key] = fresh_data.get(key, 'n/a')  # Updates and restores 'n/a' if key is absent in the socket
                # response, present --> 'key: 'n/a'' instead.'
        except AttributeError:  # 'str' object has no attribute 'keys'
            print('No Data')
            return
        except (ValueError, KeyError) as error:
            sys.stderr.write(str(error))  # Look for extra data in stream
            return


As you see this last ValueError, KeyError throw a error with extraneous data that is in the JSON object shipped from GPSD

> [io@io gps3] :( python3 human.py -host 192.168.0.4
Keyboard interrupt received
Terminated by user
Good Bye.
.
Extra data: line 1 column 2 - line 2 column 1 (char 1 - 15)Extra data: line 1 column 5 - line 2 column 1 (char 4 - 512)Extra data: line 1 column 2 - line 2 column 1 (char 1 - 97)Extra data: line 1 column 2 - line 2 column 1 (char 1 - 113)Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)Extra data: line 1 column 2 - line 2 column 1 (char 1 - 159)Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)[io@io gps3] :( 

I'm looking for a way to join this data with the data that is already in the buffer.  I'm also open to ideas.

issue_title: Discussion: Test util for mocking time.sleep()
issue_body:
The way retrying is written and captures time.sleep() in a closure within Retrying.__init__(), consumers cannot patch time.sleep() in their UT code.

To get around this I've been doing some ad-hoc mocking like [1]. While this works I have to replicate it in each openstack project.

Is there some way to can devise for consumers to more easily mockout time.sleep() for their UTs? Maybe there's an easy way to do it as-is and I'm missing it.

[1] https://review.openstack.org/#/c/368235/2/cinder/tests/unit/test_utils.py@38

issue_title: Rationalize T and Path differences
issue_body:
At first, T and Path seem like they have a lot of overlap. But T is for very specific access, and Path() is for more looser, more general access. It doesn't look like either is going away, or merging into the other and that's good because that means this is all we need to do:

* Allow `T` objects inside of `Path`, such that `Path(T)` gives the same result as `Path()` (See note below)
* Make `_get_path` support resolving Ts
* Eliminate `GlomKeyError` and so forth, opting instead for the PathAccessError throughout. 

The one disadvantage is that users won't be able to use `T` as keys in their own dict targets, but that seems a very very niche case. The one challenge is making the PathAccessError messaging around the "index" of the path with the problem reflect the `T` traversal.

issue_title: CharEnumField throws TypeError during instantiation
issue_body:
Using a `CharEnumField` throws a `TypeError` when it attempts to index into the enum dictionary keys, which are `dict_keys` objects in python3 instead of lists.

I'm using scapy-python3 version 0.18 and python 3.5.1

Traceback:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "bug.py", line 3, in <module>
    class SomeLayer(Packet):
  File "bug.py", line 5, in SomeLayer
    CharEnumField("foo", "X", {"X": 1, "Y": 2})
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/scapy/fields.py", line 766, in __init__
    if k and len(k[0]) != 1:
TypeError: 'dict_keys' object does not support indexing
```

Snippet to reproduce:
```
from scapy.all import *

class SomeLayer(Packet):
    fields_desc = [
        CharEnumField("foo", "X", {"X": 1, "Y": 2})
    ]
```

Converting the keys to a list first seems like a straightforward fix, i.e.:
````
class CharEnumField(EnumField):
    def __init__(self, name, default, enum, fmt = "1s"):
        EnumField.__init__(self, name, default, enum, fmt)
        k = list(self.i2s.keys())
        if k and len(k[0]) != 1:
            self.i2s,self.s2i = self.s2i,self.i2s
````

issue_title: Comply with E402: module level import not at top of file
issue_body:
[migrate.py:15-23](https://github.com/kofrasa/migrate/blob/0.3.7/migrate.py#L15)
According to PEP8, all module level imports should be on top of the file.

See [Code lay-out](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#imports):
> Imports are always put at the top of the file, just after any module comments and docstrings, and before module globals and constants.
> 
> Imports should be grouped in the following order:
> 
>     standard library imports
>     related third party imports
>     local application/library specific imports
> 
> You should put a blank line between each group of imports.
> 
> Put any relevant `__all__` specification after the imports.
>

issue_title: new iterutils.set_path function
issue_body:
Like `get_path`, but for setting. I needed it myself so I wrote a simple version. If this is something you think you'd like added, I can flesh it out with more error handling, documentation, tests, etc.

```python
def set_path(root, path, value):
    """
    Sets a value inside a nested object via a tuple representing the lookup
    path.
    """
    cur = root
    for seg in path[:-1]:
        cur = cur[seg]
    cur[path[-1]] = value

issue_title: port is incorrectly read from ssh config file as a string, leading to AssertionError: PORT is not a number
issue_body:
When initializing SSHTunnelForwarder() with the default ssh config file, an AssertionError is raised:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test_ssh.py", line 99, in <module>
    remote_bind_address=(mysql_host, int(mysql_port))) as server:
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/sshtunnel.py", line 877, in __init__
    check_port(self.ssh_port)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/sshtunnel.py", line 76, in check_port
    assert isinstance(port, int), 'PORT is not a number'
AssertionError: PORT is not a number

Looking through the code, it seems that in _read_ssh_config(), ssh_port is taken "as is" from hostname_info. hostname_info, in turn, is the result of paramiko.SSHConfig().lookup() which returns port as string. Thus int assertion fails on ssh_port.

I was able to fix the issue by simply converting ssh_port to int in _read_ssh_config():
sshtunnel.py, line 911: ssh_port = ssh_port or hostname_info.get('port')
change to 
sshtunnel.py, line 911: ssh_port = ssh_port or int(hostname_info.get('port'))

Let me know if I'm missing something. Thanks.

issue_title: Interpreter: completions from namespaces all have type=='module'
issue_body:
When using the Interpreter() API, all completions have the type 'module'.

The problem seems to be in Interpreter._simple_complete(self, path, like)    
```python
...
completions = []
for namespace in namespaces:
  for name in dir(namespace):
    if name.lower().startswith(like.lower()):
      scope = self._parser.module()
      n = FakeName(name, scope)
      completions.append((n, scope))
return completions
```
because each FakeName Completion will have the parser module as scope, so the type will be 'module'.
 
If I patch the method like below, I get better types, but it is unclear to me if using CompiledObject()
is always the right thing...

```python
...
completions = []
for namespace in namespaces:
for name in dir(namespace):
   if name.lower().startswith(like.lower()):
     parserScope = self._parser.module()
     scope = compiled.CompiledObject(getattr(namespace, name), parserScope)
     n = FakeName(name, scope)
     completions.append((n, scope))
return completions
```

issue_title: Jedi could not find modules in eggs
issue_body:
I stumbled upon this several days ago when tried to look up a freshly installed module that came in a wheel package.  The problem seems to be that pre33-module finder uses `imp` module that does not support eggs:

https://github.com/davidhalter/jedi/blob/fbe26ab64a75753eb4f3baa268e8bfd3b4310a7c/jedi/_compatibility.py#L57

It could be improved by reimplementing module lookup mechanism using ``pkgutil.ImpLoader`` for regular modules and ``zipimport.zipimporter`` for eggs.  And during reimplementation we can make it work on custom lists, not necessarily `sys.path`, so the `sys.path` swap trick will become unnecessary, too.

issue_title: Shebang interpreter should point to python3
issue_body:
Since **pywal** is only compatible with python>=3.6 the script should be instructed to run using `python3` instead of `python`. 

See [PEP 394](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0394/)

> # Abstract
> This PEP provides a convention to ensure that Python scripts can continue to be portable across *nix systems, regardless of the default version of the Python interpreter (i.e. the version invoked by the `python` command).
> * `python2` will refer to some version of Python 2.x.
> * `python3` will refer to some version of Python 3.x.
> * for the time being, all distributions should ensure that `python` refers to the same target as `python2`.
> * however, end users should be aware that `python` refers to `python3` on at least Arch Linux (that change is what prompted the creation of this PEP), so `python` should be used in the shebang line only for scripts that are source compatible with both Python 2 and 3.
> * in preparation for an eventual change in the default version of Python, Python 2 only scripts should either be updated to be source compatible with Python 3 or else to use `python2` in the shebang line.

issue_title: [Feature request] Git integration
issue_body:
Right now I have a couple of aliase to speed up using git on my dotdrop repository:
```
alias dotdrop='~/git/dotfiles/dotdrop.sh'
alias dotgit='git -C ~/git/dotfiles'
alias dotsync='dotgit pull; dotgit add -A; dotgit commit; dotgit push && dotdrop install'
```
Might not be the best naming scheme but the idea is that when I edit a dotfile on any of my pc's, I can run dotsync on the originating pc, write a commit message. Then I can just run dotsync again everywhere else and it automagically syncs and installs everything with a grand total of two commands.

I think end users would really benefit from git integration like this. I have no idea how I would start implementing something like this but I'm just putting this on the table for the contributors to consider.

issue_title: test multiprocessing.pool.Pool.apply with coverage is too slow
issue_body:
`the` simple testcase here

```
# coding: utf-8


from multiprocessing.pool import Pool
import time


current_milli_time = lambda: int(round(time.time() * 1000))


class Master(object):
    def __init__(self, processes=1):
        self.processes = processes
        self.pool = Pool(self.processes)


    def handle(self):
        start_time = current_milli_time()
        result = self.pool.apply(worker, (start_time, ), {})
        return result


def worker(start_time):
    return current_milli_time() - start_time


class TestPool(object):
    def test_pool(self):
        m = Master()
        print m.handle()
        assert False
```

issue_title: Relatively high CPU usage
issue_body:
This might be a low priority issue but is there any way that  the CPU usage of glances can be cut down? It's not usually an issue for me since, at most, it seems to take between 10% and 20% CPU across differing hardware but fluctuates every few seconds and usually drops a bit lower. The only times where I see this to be more of a problem is when I'm monitoring servers that are under very high loads.

For example, lets say I'm looking at a server with 40 cores, 128 GB RAM, and 4 SSDs in a RAID setup. This server is hit incredibly hard because it's sole purpose is to host a RDBMS. It would be nice to not have glances suck up as much CPU as it does now so there's more available to the RDBMS that really needs it.

I should note that I'm always running glances with the system default python which is 2.x (I'm either using it on RHEL, CentOS 6/7, or some version of Ubuntu Server). Is there a noticeable improvement when using this with Python 3.x instead of 2.x?

issue_title: Memory usage for reading of 268 MiB
issue_body: My feeling is that lasio is using too much memory. Please find a test program that I wrote to monitor the memory usage: https://github.com/VelizarVESSELINOV/DockerTest I created a dummy LAS file with 0.5M rows and 51 mnemonics the result is 267.98 MiB file which is reasonable size (without being very large) for LAS file. My expected maximum memory usage is numpy.ndarray which is most probably 1/8 of the LAS ASCII file. I didn't expect the raw ASCII file to be put in memory. The result of ```pympler``` after ```lasio.read```: ``` Reading file types | # objects | total size ===================================== | =========== | ============ The Python ```memory_profiler``` is giving line-by-line memory consumption of 500 MiB per read. I have the feeling that they are not capturing the numpy allocations that are done in C or Fortan. ``` Line # Mem usage Increment Line Contents ================================================ 31 27.8 MiB 0.0 MiB @profile 32 def file_test(rows=500000, cols=50): 33 "File test" 34 27.8 MiB 0.0 MiB print("Creating file with {} rows and {} columns".format(rows, cols)) 35 88.4 MiB 60.6 MiB file = create_file(rows, cols) 36 88.4 MiB 0.0 MiB print("Size of the file: {:.2f} MiB".format(getsize(file) / (1024 * 1024))) 37 88.4 MiB 0.0 MiB print("Reading file") 38 64.0 MiB -24.4 MiB sum1 = summarize(get_objects()) 39 551.9 MiB 487.9 MiB las = read(file) 40 524.4 MiB -27.5 MiB sum2 = summarize(get_objects()) 41 524.4 MiB 0.0 MiB diff = get_diff(sum1, sum2) 42 524.4 MiB 0.0 MiB print_(diff) 43 44 529.3 MiB 4.9 MiB for curve in las.curves: 45 529.3 MiB 0.0 MiB print("Name: {}, Min: {:.2f}, Mean: {:.2f}, Max: {:.2f}" 46 529.3 MiB 0.0 MiB .format(curve.mnemonic, nanmin(curve.data), nanmean(curve.data), 47 529.3 MiB 0.0 MiB nanmax(curve.data))) 48 49 257.3 MiB -272.0 MiB del las 50 752.4 MiB 495.1 MiB las = read(file) 51 285.9 MiB -466.5 MiB del las 52 757.6 MiB 471.7 MiB las = read(file) 53 290.0 MiB -467.5 MiB del las 54 756.4 MiB 466.4 MiB las = read(file) 55 287.6 MiB -468.8 MiB del las 56 287.6 MiB 0.0 MiB print("Happy end") ``` The ```psutil``` process activity profiler is giving the plot below for 5 seconds interval: ![psutilprofile](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/8209940/9432651/e8a8f888-49da-11e5-9af3-d241469b7e43.png) - cpu: in yellow almost 100% all the time (normal) - rss: in magenda for Resident Set Size (equivalent to Mem Usage on Windows) - vms: in dark blue for Virtual Memory Size The plot is generated by https://github.com/VelizarVESSELINOV/DockerTest/blob/master/process_log.py More than 2 GiB of memory usage for reading less than 0.3 GiB file it the opposite ratio that I expect. I really like the library but for large file I need to use very expensive large memory nodes/laptop or you will be able to optimize the reading memory usage. Please try to optimize the the reading memory usage as I mentioned in issue #50 with ```numpy.fromiter``` and please check with the code to reduce the memory usage. I done in the past several large CSV file readers in Python and managed to read several GiB of files without problems.

Sort of a follow-on from #71, which prompted me to dig deeper into how we were not really compatible with Sphinx 1.5/1.6.

Sphinx 1.5 was just due to Python 2.6 being dropped, so we did that here too, and no problem.

Sphinx 1.6 made a lot of internal API changes that broke us. I fixed all the stuff that seems to pop up in our test suites, but there's a side channel - parsing the changelog 'in-process' for stuff like my release Invoke tasks (which need to answer questions about a project's changelog state.)

Specifically, something seems to be almost skipping Sphinx itself when rendering, leaving us with "I'm docutils and what is this???" errors like:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6088/31794592-ac84acc0-b4d7-11e7-9e66-b740e35eb8ce.png)

Well...clearly `:doc:` should freaking work in Sphinx-flavored RST! (It also complained about a `:ref:` that I temporarily commented out.)

issue_title: Einsum broadcast regression
issue_body:
It was pointed out on the main Numpy repository that the current python-based parsing tech misses a few broadcasting operations and tries to run them through BLAS routines which does not work.

See the issue [here](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/10343) and PR to fix it [here](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/10352). This patch needs to be pulled over to opt_einsum before the next release.

issue_title: Run against a specific server without downloading the full server list.
issue_body:
`speedtest-cli --list` times out a lot. I'm in Vietnam, so my connection is rough sometimes, but I figure that's when this tool is most useful.

I'm writing a little bash script to results into a `csv` file on every test run so you can see at a glance wifi network name, speed, date of test and so on. It tries to hit a server in San Francisco so that it tests the cross-pacific connection speed.

`speedtest-cli --server 5754` is cool, but it always tries to download the server list before running the actual test, and this often times out. That isn't so cool.

Is there a way to run the test against a specific server without downloading the whole server list? Is this what the `--source` option is for? It's not explained in the docs, and nowhere is the IP for server 5754 printed.

Here's the bash script, in case you're interested in what I'm up to:

```bash
date="$(date +"%Y-%m-%d")"
time="$(date +"%l:%M%p")"
dest=5754
destination="San Francisco [$dest]"
# Split a string at the delimeter 'Network ' into variables 1, 2, etc.
wifi=`networksetup -getairportnetwork en0 | awk -F'Network: ' '{printf $2}'`
outputFile=~/Desktop/sf-speedtest-output.csv

# -------------------------------------------- Say what we're about to do
echo "$wifi --> $destination"
echo "Running speedtest-cli..."

# -------------------------------------------- Start the Test
# Run a command, printing to stdout while it runs, but also saving the output to a variable
exec 5>&1
output=$(speedtest-cli --server $dest | tee >(cat - >&5))

# Parse the output for useful data
down=`echo "$output" | awk -F'Download: ' '{printf $2}'`
down=`echo "$down" | awk -F' ' '{printf $1}'`
up=`echo "$output" | awk -F'Upload: ' '{printf $2}'`
up=`echo "$up" | awk -F' ' '{printf $1}'`
ping=`echo "$output" | awk -F'km]: ' '{printf $2}'`
ping=`echo "$ping" | awk -F' ms' '{printf $1}'`
echo "Speeds: $down/$up mbps. Ping: $ping ms."


# -------------------------------------------- Save to File
# File Format: wifi, downspeed, upspeed, date, time, destination, ping
echo "$wifi,$down,$up,$date,$time,$destination,$ping" >> $outputFile
echo "Test results saved to $outputFile"
```

issue_title: Timeouts don't seem to be working
issue_body:
I've written (an admittedly torturous) test to test out how well pickling works with a chunk of code I have.  The relevant section is below.

```python
with Settings(timeout=-1):
    @given(max_speed=float,
           tradingpost_probability=float,
           pause_mean=float,
           max_step_size=float,
           name=str,
           robot_call_periodicity=float)
    def test_pickling(self, max_speed, tradingpost_probability, pause_mean, 
                      max_step_size, name, robot_call_periodicity):
        assume(max_speed >= 0.0)
        assume(tradingpost_probability >= 0.0)
        assume(tradingpost_probability <= 1.0)
        assume(pause_mean > 0.0)
        assume(max_step_size > 0.0)
        assume(robot_call_periodicity > 0.0)
        assume(robot_call_periodicity < 1000.0)
```

This fails with:
> hypothesis.errors.Unsatisfiable: Unable to satisfy assumptions of hypothesis test_pickling. Only 4 examples found after 0.121668 seconds

The problem is the timeouts.  Reading through the documentation of ``core.find_satisfying_template()``, it appears that by setting the ``timeout`` setting to a negative number, hypothesis should no longer timeout; that is, it will work for as long as is necessary to generate enough examples.  However, what actually happens is that the timeouts are still defaulting to whatever the defaults are.  Is this a bug in hypothesis, or is this an error on my part?

Thanks, and thanks for writing such a fantastic library!

issue_title: Lifecycle API
issue_body:
One of the things you need for testing is a bunch of hooks into the life-cycle – setup and teardown for example. Hypothesis currently has the executors API which nobody including me likes. It’s a bit weird, very limited, and ties you to class based testing.

I’d like to expose a detailed lifecycle API that lets you hook into a number of events. In particular I need to be able to insert code around just the test body (executors currently treat value creation and test execution identically). Combining this with the better @given configuration above makes it easy to ditch the dependence on class based testing.

I’d still like to be able to support some sort of auto derivation of life cycle events for class based tests (unittest setup and tear down for example).

I’d also like to integrate this with py.test function level fixtures, but that’s currently impossible.

With the new [template based rendering](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/releases/1.11/) of django 1.11, the choice field renderer class has been removed ([source](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/releases/1.11/#changes-due-to-the-introduction-of-template-based-widget-rendering)).

This stops projects using django angular from even running the development server since [it is used to add ng-form to choice fields](https://github.com/jrief/django-angular/blob/master/djng/forms/widgets.py#L44)

issue_title: Grant code is not completely validated
issue_body:
When the client exchanges their short lived token (grant code) for a long lived one (access token/refresh token), the grant code must be checked to ensure that it matches what the oauthlib had sent them.

Currently, this works:
**1. Get authorization code:**
```
POST oauth/authorize?client_id=valid_id
                     &response_type=code
                     &scope=all
```
Response:
```json
{
    "code": "jWnNvGgIDSyfRhkTNSRCyRElR373ip"
}
```
**2. Exchange authorization code for an access token:**
```
POST oauth/token?client_id=valid_id
                 &client_secret=valid_secret
                 &grant_type=authorization_code
                 &redirect_url=oauth/redirect_uri
                 &code=INVALID_CODE_THAT_DOESNT_MATCH_THE_ONE_FROM_STEP_1
```
Response:
```json
{
    "token_type": "Bearer",
    "version": "0.1.0",
    "access_token": "2Kz2MKZnCjXMJTbgcZyiJrxf8wP9kk",
    "scope": "all",
    "expires_in": 3600,
    "refresh_token": "lnN8cURwQhTJSWzMSiLh5DJFeTrqur"
}
```



Step 2 should fail with 
```json
{
    "error": "invalid_grant"
}
```
since the code that was submitted in step 2 doesn't match the code in the response from step 1.

issue_title: Less-Tempfiles are within package dir
issue_body:
The less Tempfiles are created as
```python
# path to save tempfile with style_less before reading/compiling
tempfile = os.path.join(package_dir, 'tempfile.less')
vimtemp = os.path.join(package_dir, 'vimtemp.less')
```
This is a problem on some linux distributions, as the package dir will be in `/usr/lib/` and thus write protected.

The quick fix `sudo jt [...]` doesn't work, as the themes will then be installed into the root user's home directory.

Is there any reason we're not using `tempfile.mkstemp` from the standard library?

issue_title: Deleting a related object still stores the related object as a version in the latest revision.
issue_body:
Hi,

I am encountering an issue when deleting an object still shows in the latest revision of a model in the database.

Steps:
1. I add a foreign key object to my model. This shows up in the newly created revision, as expected.
2. I delete the foreign key object, but it still shows up in the newly created revision.
3. I make an arbitrary change to my model e.g. change a text field. The foreign key object that I showed earlier is now not shown in this new revision.

I'd expect that once you delete a foreign key object, it should not appear in the revision created during that operation.

I am using the https://github.com/jedie/django-reversion-compare project, and when I carry out the above steps I can't ever see what has been deleted until I make a further arbitrary change to the model, where it shows the arbitrary change and the foreign key deletion. It seems to be an issue with the base django-reversion package.

Environment: 
Django==1.9.6
django-reversion==2.0.8
django-reversion-compare==0.7.2
djangorestframework==3.4.7

I am using the RevisionMixin on a Django REST Framework ViewSet CBV to enable the django-revision functionality.

Has anyone experienced this behaviour? Thanks!

See https://github.com/striglia/pyramid_swagger/blob/master/tox.ini#L2 for where we disabled testing.

We got a bug report that running 2to3 on the repo complained about this line:
```
File "/Users/.../.virtualenvs/.../lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyramid_swagger/tween.py", line 418
raise exc_class(str(exc)), None, sys.exc_info()[2]
```

Short term, we need to remove py3x trove classifiers from setup.py.
To complete this ticket, we need to:

* fix any existing non-3-compatible issues in the repo
* re-enable py3x testing in tox.ini
* put py3x trove classifiers back into setup.py

issue_title: Skipped months for 'last day of the month' case (freq=monthly, bymonthday=31,-1)
issue_body:
**Use case:** when I set start to 31/12/17 and end to 31/12/18 I want to get occurrences in every month, on 30th's in months with 30 days and on 28th for February. 

dateutil doc says:

> rrule(MONTHLY, dtstart=datetime(2007,1,31))  
> This would only generate values for 31st Jan, 31st Mar, 31st May, etc. Any month with less than 31 days is ignored.
> To generate a rrule for the use case of "a date on the specified day of the month, unless it is beyond the end of month, in which case it will be the last day of the month" use the following:
> rrule(MONTHLY, bymonthday=(some_day, -1), bysetpos=1)

**Problem:** after using these params still only occurrences in months with 31 days are returned.

**Possible fix:**
in models.events._event_params there is a condition "if sp == ..." which overwrites list of param values with list with only the first value. It should (?) be splitted into two cases like this:

`if sp == rule_params[param]:
                    event_params[param] = [sp]
                elif hasattr(rule_params[param], '__iter__') and \
                        sp in rule_params[param]:
                    event_params[param] = rule_params[param]
                else:
                    event_params[param] = rule_params[param]`

Unless there is a really good reason to overwrite values lists...?

issue_title: Support for Django 2.0
issue_body:
django-url-filter uses some features that have been [deprecated with django version 2.0](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/releases/2.0/#features-removed-in-2-0)

issue_title: FileForm field widget not working
bands around predicting survival (CoxPH)
issue_body:
**Context**
For a CoxPH model with N members (aka patients), each member m has their own survival curve S_m(t): = S_m(t; betas(m)). We know S_m(t) is derived from the global baseline survival curve S(t) and the individual m's risk = sum_{f in features} m(f).beta(f). 

**Question**
Is there an appropriate definition of the variance of S_m(t) that may be added to Lifelines?

Of course we may calculate the global (baseline) variance for all N members via Var(S(t)) = S(t)*(1-S(t)) / N, but I wonder whether it makes sense to add Var(S(t)), as opposed to some Var(S_m(t)), to Lifelines. See the potential Application below.

**Application**
If m has already survived t_0 days, then E_m: = E(future days(m) | t_0) = int_{t_0}^{infty} S_m(t) dt / S_m(t_0). It would then be nice to formulate an optimistic/conservative estimate of E_m by translating S_m(t) by +/- k.std(m), where k is an integer (eg k=2) and std(m) is some 'standard deviation' of S_m(t).

disable_properties:false,

issue_title: Django 2.0 support: _format_value renamed, maybe more
issue_body:
Django 2.0 is slated for release in about a month and django-phonenumber-field should work with it.

As far as I know, django-phonenumber-field uses at least one feature that had a breaking change in Django 2.0: [Widget.format_value](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/forms/widgets/#django.forms.Widget.format_value) has been renamed from `_format_value` to `format_value` to make it a part of the public API.

The CI test matrix is growing quite large, and with the addition of Python 3.6 and Django 2.0 it will become even larger. Django upstream suggests to drop support for anything older than 1.11 LTS and make sure the project runs on 1.11 without any warnings. Then it should work on 2.x as well without changes. I'll leave to the project maintainers to decide if the list of Django versions supported by django-phonenumber-field should be pruned a bit.

issue_title: Find models' definition of "python" style in Model Zoo in Gluon Package
issue_body:
How can I get the model's python definition in Gluon Package? Not only json file. For example, for vgg16 as: 

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
def vgg16_symbol():
    data = mx.sym.Variable('data')
    
    # conv1
    conv1_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=data, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=64, name="conv1_1")
    relu1_1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv1_1, act_type="relu", name="relu1_1")
    conv1_2 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu1_1, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=64, name="conv1_2")
    relu1_2 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv1_2, act_type="relu", name="relu1_2")
    pool1   = mx.sym.Pooling(data=relu1_2, kernel=(2, 2), stride=(2, 2), pool_type="max", name="pool1")
    
    # conv2
    conv2_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=pool1, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=128, name="conv2_1")
    relu2_1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv2_1, act_type="relu", name="relu2_1")
    conv2_2 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu2_1, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=128, name="conv2_2")
    relu2_2 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv2_2, act_type="relu", name="relu2_2")
    pool2   = mx.sym.Pooling(data=relu2_2, kernel=(2, 2), stride=(2, 2), pool_type="max", name="pool2")
    
    # conv3
    conv3_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=pool2, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=256, name="conv3_1")
    relu3_1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv3_1, act_type="relu", name="relu3_1")
    conv3_2 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu3_1, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=256, name="conv3_2")
    relu3_2 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv3_2, act_type="relu", name="relu3_2")
    conv3_3 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu3_2, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=256, name="conv3_3")
    relu3_3 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv3_3, act_type="relu", name="relu3_3")
    pool3   = mx.sym.Pooling(data=relu3_3, kernel=(2, 2), stride=(2, 2), pool_type="max", name="pool3")
    
    # conv4
    conv4_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=pool3, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=512, name="conv4_1")
    relu4_1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv4_1, act_type="relu", name="relu4_1")
    conv4_2 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu4_1, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=512, name="conv4_2")
    relu4_2 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv4_2, act_type="relu", name="relu4_2")
    conv4_3 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu4_2, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=512, name="conv4_3")
    relu4_3 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv4_3, act_type="relu", name="relu4_3")
    pool4   = mx.sym.Pooling(data=relu4_3, kernel=(2, 2), stride=(2, 2), pool_type="max", name="pool4")
    
    # conv5
    conv5_1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=pool4, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=512, name="conv5_1")
    relu5_1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv5_1, act_type="relu", name="relu5_1")
    conv5_2 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu5_1, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=512, name="conv5_2")
    relu5_2 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv5_2, act_type="relu", name="relu5_2")
    conv5_3 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=relu5_2, kernel=(3, 3), pad=(1, 1), num_filter=512, name="conv5_3")
    relu5_3 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv5_3, act_type="relu", name="relu5_3")
    pool5   = mx.sym.Pooling(data=relu5_3, kernel=(2, 2), stride=(2, 2), pool_type="max", name="pool5")
    
    # fc6
    flat6     = mx.sym.Flatten(data=pool5, name="flat6")
    fc6     = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=flat6, num_hidden=4096, name="fc6")
    relu6   = mx.sym.Activation(data=fc6, act_type="relu", name="relu6")
    drop6   = mx.sym.Dropout(data=relu6, p=0.5, name="drop6")
    
    # fc7
    fc7     = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=drop6, num_hidden=4096, name="fc7")
    relu7   = mx.sym.Activation(data=fc7, act_type="relu", name="relu7")
    drop7   = mx.sym.Dropout(data=relu7, p=0.5, name="drop7")
    
    # fc8
    fc8     = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=drop7, num_hidden=1000, name="fc8")
    softmax = mx.sym.Softmax(data=fc8, name="softmax")
    
    return softmax
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

I need this kind of file, because it is convenient for me to modify the network architecture, not just use the  network simply. Moreover, it is also convenient to check the name and other parameters of each layer.

For example, if I want to modify the "resnet152_v2" in model zoo in Gluon Package, how should I do except inefficiently rewriting it's symbol in python by myself ?

My ideas:
1. load the resnet152_v2 using Gluon.model_zoo API and save as json file, then convert the json file to .py file with "def resnet152_v2_symbol():" in it.  But, I don't know how to do the second step.


Any solutions ? 
Thanks.

issue_title: How to build a library without the BLAS dependency?
issue_body:
As I saw from mxnet document "Deep Learning in a Single File for Smart Devices", there is a word saying "MXNet provides an amalgamation script that compiles all code needed for prediction based on trained DL models into a single .cc file, containing approximately 30K lines of code. This code only depends on the BLAS library. Moreover, we’ve also created an even more minimal version, with the BLAS dependency removed."

But I didn't find any description about how to build the minimal version that without BLAS dependency. Could anyone tell me anything about this? Any help is appreciated, thanks!

issue_title: why share the same value of class member when using multigpu of HybridBlock, bug?
issue_body:
## Description
in my opinion, when using multi-gpu for training, each device should do forward() on one device(gpu) independently, and it should use it's own class member value, not share class member value cross devices. Is this correct?  

but i found when using HybridBlock, multi devices share the same value, why?

ubuntu16.04
python2.7.13

MXNet commit hash:
```deb25bca9803cac338faedb2eb14dfda22813b75```

## Minimum reproducible example
```python
# mnist_bug.py
import argparse,logging
import numpy as np
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import gluon, autograd
from mxnet.gluon import nn

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)
batch_size = 128
epochs = 10

class BugBlock(gluon.HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self):
        super(BugBlock, self).__init__()
        self.cnt = 0
    def hybrid_forward(self, F, x):
        self.cnt += 1
        print("ctx={}\tcnt={}".format(x.context, self.cnt))
        return x

net = nn.HybridSequential()
with net.name_scope():
    net.add(nn.Dense(1280, activation='relu'))
    net.add(nn.Dense(640, activation='relu'))
    net.add(nn.Dense(10))
    net.add(BugBlock())

def transformer(data, label):
    data = data.reshape((-1,)).astype(np.float32)/255
    return data, label

train_data = gluon.data.DataLoader(
    gluon.data.vision.MNIST('./data', train=True, transform=transformer),
    batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True, last_batch='discard')

def train(epochs, ctx):
    net.initialize(ctx=ctx)
    trainer = gluon.Trainer(net.collect_params(), 'sgd', {'learning_rate': 0.1, 'momentum': 0.9})
    metric = mx.metric.Accuracy()
    loss = gluon.loss.SoftmaxCrossEntropyLoss()
    for epoch in range(epochs):
        metric.reset()
        for i, (data, label) in enumerate(train_data):
            data = gluon.utils.split_and_load(data, ctx_list=ctx, batch_axis=0)
            label = gluon.utils.split_and_load(label, ctx_list=ctx, batch_axis=0)
            outputs = []
            Ls = []
            with autograd.record():
                for x, y in zip(data, label):
                    z = net(x)
                    L = loss(z, y)
                    Ls.append(L)
                    outputs.append(z)
                for L in Ls:
                    L.backward()
            trainer.step(data[0].shape[0])
            metric.update(label, outputs)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    ctx = [mx.gpu(0), mx.gpu(1)]
    train(epochs, ctx)

```

## Steps to reproduce
run ```python mnist_bug.py```, outputs log will like this:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5860892/34650916-cea30994-f403-11e7-8f0a-0e83373be994.png)

which `cnt` is share like a static variable.

@szha @piiswrong

issue_title: Plugins support
issue_body:
I see a plugin system's primary purpose in providing standardized extension points for operators of Luigi-based scheduling infrastructure.

Some of the primary purposes for plugins:
* Register logging handlers
* Register [event](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/event.py) handlers
* Initialize and clean up ad-hoc resource management
* Register utility Tasks to be available during CLI parsing before the user-provided `--module` has been parsed out and imported? (Unsure, maybe `--module`(s) should be parsed in a separate pass and utilities that the user uses should be explicitly imported in the user code...)

Currently the approach we use for achieving the above is to wrap `luigi` in a proprietary wrapper executable, which is confusing and doesn't compose.

Related issues have been filed previously:
* https://github.com/spotify/luigi/issues/1088

issue_title: Calculation is not stopped when a process is killed/terminated
issue_body:
When one of the subprocesses is killed (by the user, by the OOM killer) the computation isn't stopped and other processes continues to crunch data until the tasks have been completed.

This is quite dangerous because it leaves several processes alive using resources (or even idle but with memory allocated) and in the case of OOM killer intervention the situation get works because of the high memory pressure (i.e OOM kills one process base on scoring but this does not stop other processes to leak, depending on the memory usage rate, the OOM killer may be too slow).

One solution could be (on unix) using:
`os.killpg(os.getpgrp(), signal.SIGKILL)` in `raiseMasterKilled`

[Feature request] Ability to fetch full cloudwatch logs from boto after lambda invoke
issue_body:
I would really like to be able to fetch cloudwatch logs from boto3. 
As in, I want a way to fetch in python what I can see at:

    https://ap-southeast-2.console.aws.amazon.com/cloudwatch/home?region=ap-southeast-2#logEventViewer:group=/aws/lambda/my_lambda_name;start=PT5M

As far as I can tell, there's no way to do this with boto3.

I can get close by looking at `base64.b64decode(response['LogResult'])` after calling `response = boto3.client('lambda', config=config).invoke(...)`.

However those logs are only a subset of the actual logs.

The specific case I'm dealing with is looking at logs of lambdas which execute python `nose` tests. In the web GUI for CloudWatch I see:

* Traceback (exception captured, handled and printed by nose, I don't know exactly how);
* Logger output; then
* stdout

With `base64.b64decode(response['LogResult'])` I only see stdout, not the other 2.

Is there a way to see the other 2 from boto3? If not, I would like there to be. My current workaround is to run `client.invoke()` and then generate the aforementioned url so the user can copy and paste it into their browser. This is a pain because (aside from being manual), when multiple lambdas are invoked in parallel, the logs become very confusing, and it takes a while to find what I'm actually looking for.

In addition to modifying `client.invoke` so that it returns the full logs, I think it would be generally useful to add a function to the CloudWatch client so that you can look up the logs for a particular lambda, or a particular invocation of a particular lambda.

Linux ip-10-30-1-172.ec2.internal 3.10.0-693.el7.x86_64 #1 SMP Thu Jul 6 19:56:57 EDT 2017 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux
# aws --version
aws-cli/1.14.3 Python/2.7.5 Linux/3.10.0-693.el7.x86_64 botocore/1.8.7
```

Botocore includes it's own cacert.pem file:
`/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/botocore/vendored/requests/cacert.pem`

However RHEL 7 has a centrally managed ca-bundle located at:
`/etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt`

The Python 'requests' library included with RHEL (`python-requests-2.6.0-1.el7_1.noarch`) has over-ridden the default certificate location in `certs.py` and refers to the central certificate bundle.

Contents of `/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/requests/certs.py`:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

"""
certs.py
~~~~~~~~

This module returns the preferred default CA certificate bundle.

If you are packaging Requests, e.g., for a Linux distribution or a managed
environment, you can change the definition of where() to return a separately
packaged CA bundle.

We return "/etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt" provided by the ca-certificates
package.
"""

try:
    from certifi import where
except ImportError:
    def where():
        """ Don't use the certs bundled with requests, use ca-certificates. """
        return "/etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt"

if __name__ == '__main__':
    print(where())
```

Users expect that updates made to their central ca-bundle using the 'ca-certificates' tooling would be respected by botocore.  Can botocore use the requests library that comes with the O/S?  Or can some logic be added if there is an existing requests library to use its `certs.py` routine to locate the ca-bundle?

A simple workaround at present would be a symlink:
`ln -sf /etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/botocore/vendored/requests/cacert.pem`

Thanks!

issue_title: ValueError raised instead of GitCommandError when not authenticated
issue_body:
I have a git repo that needs a key, if I don't enter the password for the key, when using Repo.clone_from or Repo.pull GitCommandError isn't raised.

```
  File "/home/stu/.virtualenvs/ng/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/git/cmd.py", line 319, in wait
    raise GitCommandError(self.args, status, self.proc.stderr.read())
ValueError: read of closed file
```

^ which is because proc.stderr is closed at this point.


To reproduce, clone from a git repo using a key + a uri like

git@bitbucket.org:blah/blah.git

Then when it prompts you to unlock the key, just press enter (not unlocking) it - similar thing for pull as well.

issue_title: Dependencies for Python 3 and PyPy support
issue_body:
pthon-u2flib-host currently only Python 2.x (in particular CPython 2.x). The limiting factor is the required libraries. Porting python-u2flib-host would first require porting, or replacing
- [M2Crypto](https://github.com/martinpaljak/M2Crypto)
- [python-hidapi](https://github.com/trezor/cython-hidapi)

I'd like to propose M2Crypto be replaced, the project appears to be stalled (with no commits since May 2015) and it's written using SWIG which [isn't compatible with PyPy](https://bitbucket.org/pypy/compatibility/wiki/Home#!swig-swig). I'd like to propose [cryptography](https://github.com/pyca/cryptography)
- Apache/BSD dual licensed
- Supports Python 2.6-2.7, Python 3.3+ and PyPy
- Tested on Windows, Mac OSX and Linux
- Ships pre-built wheels for Python 32-bit and 64-bit on Windows
- Has multiple maintainers i.e. a bus factor > 1

I'm happy to do the work and provide a PR, but first I'd like to check you're happy with the concept. Would Yubico be willing to use cryptography instead of M2Crypto for python-u2flib-host?

issue_title: simpleconsumer does not raise errors in fetch_offsets.
issue_body:
simpleconsumer is not correctly raising KafkaException in the fetch_offsets method. This is due to the following test: 
 https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L597-L598
```
        if len(parts_by_error) > 1:
            raise KafkaException(parts_by_error)
```
When the same error code returned for all partitions, such as GroupLoadInProgress.ERROR, then the length of parts_by_error will be 1, preventing the raising of KafkaException. This results in the subsequent reseting of the partition's offsets when messages are consumed.

issue_title: Losing my committed offsets
issue_body:
Hi all,

I have some strange behavior I can't quite get to the bottom of.  I have a consumer listening on a topic in a consumer group like so (I'm using Kafka 0.8.2):

```python
    client = KafkaClient(hosts=kafka_hosts)
    input_topic = client.topics[topic_in]
    consumer = input_topic.get_simple_consumer(consumer_group=group_id,
                                               auto_offset_reset=OffsetType.EARLIEST,
                                               auto_commit_enable=False)

    for message in consumer:
        # Do some work....
        consumer.commit_offsets()
```

This works great most of the time, e.g. I can restart the consumer and it picks up after the last successfully processed message.  

However, I've found that if I restart my consumer after its been idle for quite some time, it occasionally loses its mind and starts processing all the messages in the topic that have already been committed!  It's as if Kafka has reset the offsets on me.

Could it be something to do with where the offsets are being stored (not sure if they're being stored in ZooKeeper or Kafka)?  I came across this guy,

http://kafka.apache.org/documentation.html#offsetmigration

I'm using the defaults for 0.8.2...

Thanks for any tips on getting to the bottom of this!  It's hard for me to reproduce!

issue_title: 'regionprops()' with single label.
issue_body:
Hello,

The `measure.regionprops()` documentation was recently updated to reflect the fact that labels with value `0` are ignored (#1255). However, even if it's documented, isn't this behavior confusing? For example, when working on an image with a single connected region:

``` python
>>> labels = label(img, background=False)
>>> np.unique(labels)
array([-1,  0])
>>> regionprops(labels)
[]
```

This is easy to work around once you know what's going on, but it is surprising.

``` python
>>> labels[labels != -1] += 1
>>> regionprops(labels)
[<skimage.measure._regionprops._RegionProperties at 0x7fbfba613990>]
```

Regards,
Georgi

issue_title: "pycapnp==0.5.5" is not in `bindings/py/requirements.txt`
issue_body:
README.md states:

> The Python dependencies (NumPy and pycapnp) can be installed with pip:
> 
> ```
> pip install -r bindings/py/requirements.txt
> ```

But capnp is not indeed in `bindings/py/requirements.txt`, instead, it's installed by `extra_require` in `setup.py`:

```python
  setup(
    name="nupic.bindings",
    version=getVersion(),
    # This distribution contains platform-specific C++ libraries, but they are not
    # built with distutils. So we must create a dummy Extension object so when we
    # create a binary file it knows to make it platform-specific.
    ext_modules=[Extension('nupic.dummy', sources = ['dummy.c'])], 
    namespace_packages=["nupic"],
    install_requires=findRequirements(platform),
    packages=find_packages(),
    package_data={
        "nupic.proto": ["*.capnp"],
        "nupic.bindings": ["*.so", "*.pyd"],
    },
    extras_require = {"capnp": ["pycapnp==0.5.5"]},
```

Is this intended? Why?

This causes the following 2 problems:

* A new user would fail if only goes "Building from Source -> Prerequisites" to "Developer Installation -> Configure and generate C++ build files" on a whole new environment
* inconsistency between documentation and actual code

issue_title: travis: remove pypy from test matrix
Don't transmit error instances for protocol 5
issue_body:
Error handling is awkward in a number of ways:

- Detecting errors involves sniffing results in ways that seem like bug magnets.

- We're required to pickle error objects, with the security issues that entails.

   - We shouldn't need to pickle objects at the protocol level.

   - We might want to use alternate serialization formats that don't support object serialization.

Instead, we'll send dotted error class names and arguments and we'll use the async flag as an error flag in replies.

---> 25             _print(file, '    %s' % line.strip())

issue_title: Google Finance url is still incorrect in PyPI
issue_body:
As noted in other issues & PR's here, the Google Finance API recently changed from _google.com/finance/historical_ to _finance.google.com/historical_.

The [url in the GitHub repo seems to be correct](https://github.com/pydata/pandas-datareader/blob/master/pandas_datareader/google/daily.py#L34).

However, when I test, I'm still seeing the old url:

```python
>>> import pandas_datareader as pdr
>>> test = pdr.google.daily.GoogleDailyReader('')
>>> print(test.url)
http://www.google.com/finance/historical
```

I'm using `pdr.__version__` == 0.5.0, and have uninstalled and reinstalled (through conda-forge, if that matters), but am still seeing the same ambiguity.

The source of this is that the GitHub repo has the correct url, but the [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pandas-datareader#downloads) tar.gz download doesn't.

issue_title: 0.15.2 causing problems with pandas.io.data.Options
issue_body:
I finally traced a problem I was having with options downloads to changes made between version 0.15.1 and version 0.15.2. Probably easiest is just to link the question I posed on Stack Overflow, because it shows the behavior: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/29182526/trouble-with-http-request-from-google-compute-engine

Weirdly, in 0.15.2, I was consistently able to get the options data for large cap companies ('aapl', 'ge' were my typical test cases) but not for small cap companies such as 'spwr' or 'ddd'. Not sure what was changed, but it looks to me like it might have to do with the list of expiration dates or with the handling of empty tables given an expiration date. Right now, in any case, if you hit the link shown in my stack trace (http://finance.yahoo.com/q/op?s=SPWR&date=1430438400), there's an empty table for puts and only 1 call. That would be something that's more common for smaller companies, too. The other possibility is that the initial Options object isn't getting good links in the newer version.

That's about all I know about it, but reverting to 0.15.1 seems to have solved the problems I was having.

issue_title: Using results of Poisson CCD Simulator to simulate BF effect
issue_body: Craig Lage at UC Davis has developed a Poisson equation solver that computes the distortion of the pixel edges as the electrons get collected in the CCD pixels. In the DESC Hack day today, we made some progress towards integrating his code (in C++) with the C++ layer of the GalSim. We identified  that the natural way to include this functionality would be by tweaking the photon-shooting method inside the `drawImage` routine.  I will push the code after some cleanup, but this is not completely done yet.

issue_title: Little/minor proposed change in the docs for the REST API endpoints (https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/docs/SETTINGS.rst)
issue_body:
Hi,
Currently, they read as:

_REST API
connect_address: IP address and port to access the REST API.
listen: IP address and port that Patroni will listen to, to provide health-check information for HAProxy._

I suggest to change them to:

_REST API
connect_address: IP address (or hostname) and port, to access the REST API.
listen: IP address (or hostname) and port that Patroni will listen to, to provide health-check information for HAProxy (or any other load balancer capable of doing a HTTP "OPTION" or "GET" check.)_

I have this section working that way (with hostnames) without problems.  The added comment about HAProxy is because people are using other load balancers (as the http check OPTION/GET is common)

issue_title: Process for updating uap-core?
issue_body:
I'm wondering what's the process for updating `uap-python` with the latest rules from `uap-core`?It looks like @mattrobenolt usually does this - do you want update requests as an issue like this, a PR to merge?

The git submodule for `uap-core` currently points at a commit not associated with a tag in `uap-core` which suggests that we don't need to wait for a "release" from `uap-core` to update `uap-python`. Seems like it would be preferable to point at a release but makes sense that we'd want to avoid that level of coordination.

I'm asking because I recently submitted https://github.com/ua-parser/uap-core/pull/216 that was merged to master over the weekend and would like to pull it into `uap-python` so I can use it. 

Would be helpful to update the README with instructions on how to make these updates.

Thanks!

issue_title: normalize column names client-side instead of looking at the database
issue_body:
This can easily be done with a function like this:

```python
def norm_column(colname):
    """Convert a valid pandas DataFrame column name into a CARTO-supported
    PostgreSQL name"""
    normed = re.sub('[^a-z_0-9]', '_', colname.lower())
    if re.match('^[0-9]', normed):
        return '_' + normed
    return normed
```

Another option is to have all the column names on the database as quoted, but that seems like it would cause more problems than normalizing.

issue_title: Update libbson to 1.6
issue_body:
[Libbson 1.6](https://github.com/mongodb/libbson/releases/tag/1.6.0) included some major improvements to it's bson to json encoding:
- Use jsonsl instead of libyajl as our JSON parsing library, parse JSON more
strictly, fix minor parsing bugs.
- Extended JSON documents like '{"$code": "...", "$scope": {}}' are now parsed
into BSON "code" elements.
- ISO8601 dates now allow years from 0000 to 9999 inclusive. Before, years
before 1970 were prohibited.
- BSON floats and ints are now distinguished in JSON output.

issue_title: SagemakerModel.transform() doesn't use model's sagemakerClient
issue_body:
If you create a new `SageMakerModel` instance (say, with `fromModelS3Path()`), you can pass in your own `sagemakerClient`. However, when you go to use the model after it's been created, `transform()` does not use that client to send prediction requests. It appears to hardcode a `AmazonSageMakerRuntimeClientBuilder.defaultClient` instead in `RequestBatchIterator`.

Pardon my ignorance, but is there a reason that it can't just pass the `sagemakerClient` through?

issue_title: Datastore Query should work when no Client is passed
issue_body:
We are trying to use gcloud-python Datastore in Apache Beam python SDK to provide a Datastore connector. For our use case, the user will just provide a 'com.google.datastore.query.Query' and the connector will take care of reading data from Datastore. We do not want the user to deal with 'datastore.Client' directly. The way we plan to implement is something like this,

User Pseudo Code
```
project = 'myproject'
namespace='mynamespace'
query = Query(client = None, project, namespace )
query.addFilter(...)
DatastoreConnector.read(query, project, namespace)
```

SDK Connector Pseudo Code
```
def DatastoreConnector.read(query, project, namespace):
  client = datastore.Client(project, namespace)
  return query.fetch(client = client)
```

Note that we are passing the client to the query at fetch time. All this works fine when the `namespace` is not empty but when `namespace` is `None`, the code fails with an exception, `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'namespace'`. This is because the `Query` tries to get the `namespace` from `Client` even though `Client` is empty. Specifically fails [here](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/datastore/google/cloud/datastore/query.py#L89) and [here](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/datastore/google/cloud/datastore/query.py#L115)

Could these checks be changed to,

```
if not namespace:
  self._namespace = namespace
elif not client:
  self._namespace = client.namespace
```

Or is there any workaround for us?

issue_title: Make Content Metadata Import process happen within a transaction
issue_body:
## Summary

Following the merge of #1947, content metadata is imported into the main database, for now, this involves deleting all existing content metadata for a channel, which, if done during active classroom use would result in a disjointed user experience for active learners.

In order to avoid this, it would be better for the deletion and import to happen within a single transaction, so that it is committed all at once, can be cancelled/rolled back without issue, and just generally doesn't mess things about!

issue_title: Logic for latest version does not account for versions deployed from 12-1am
issue_body:
The current version of ipwb is InterPlanetary Wayback 0.2017.07.04.0020.

`runningLatestIPWB()` in util.py currently performs a pretty naive version check by first fetching the version from pypi (which unfortunately assumes it's up and available!), then sanitizes the currently installed version via:
`currentVersion = ipwbVersion.replace('.0', '.')`

For the date-based format we used for versioning, this works for all fields except the last, which sanitizes to 0.2017.7.4.020. Because this is != to the exact version string (remember: naive!), the currently installed version is out of date. This will only occur when the version is 0.X.Y.Z.0000-0.X.Y.Z.0059, i.e., 12am-1am inclusive of the first and exclusive of the second.

To continue with the naïveté until the checking gets smarter, check for one or more zeros to be replaced in the sanitization process. A simple regex might be in-order.

issue_title: Add CMS version to footer of the admin
changes in dependencies of dependencies of charm-tools have broken usage on trusty venvs
issue_body:
On Aug 16, this worked:
```
virtualenv .venv
. .venv/bin/activate
pip install charm-tools
charm version
```
```
charm unavailable
charm-tools 2.1.3
```

As of Aug 17, it doesn't:
```
virtualenv .venv
. .venv/bin/activate
pip install charm-tools
charm version
```
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/venv/bin/charm-version", line 5, in <module>
    from pkg_resources import load_entry_point
  File "/tmp/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 2720, in <module>
    parse_requirements(__requires__), Environment()
  File "/tmp/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 592, in resolve
    raise VersionConflict(dist,req) # XXX put more info here
pkg_resources.VersionConflict: (setuptools 2.2 (/tmp/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages), Requirement.parse('setuptools>=11.3'))
```
This appears to be because secretstorage revved up on Aug 17:
https://pypi.python.org/pypi/SecretStorage

And the reqs lineage goes like so:
`cryptography->secretstorage->keyring->launchpadlib->charm-tools`

Setting an upper constraint for secretstorage seems to resolve the issue:
```
virtualenv venv
. .venv/bin/activate
pip install 'secretstorage<2.3.0'
pip install charm-tools
charm version
```
```
charm unavailable
charm-tools 2.1.3
```


I am using: Trusty

## Issue/Feature

### I expect/expected the following

Charm-tools should successfully install in a virtualenv on Trusty.


### What I got

Charm-tools has a dependency of a dependency that has changed, causing virtualenv charm-tools installations to break on Trusty.

issue_title: What is the right way to compile a package for use with project?
issue_body:
I am trying to upload an executable paper and my code depends on two external packages (METEOR and fasttext in particular). Both of these do not distribute executables and require you to compile them from source. Unfortunately, these produce errors because the git repo folders are read-only bundles.

How one should manage this (rather common) scenario?

Some options I can see are:
1. Bake these packages into the Docker image.
2. Upload the compiled executables and hope that it works on the target machine (which comes an associated question: how does one upload a file and preserve its executable permissions)
3. Change the source files to allow specification of a target output directory

## Reproducing the error for fastText
I have uploaded the fasttext repo onto CodaLab using `cl upload -g https://github.com/epfml/sent2vec.git`.

I then try to compile it using `cl run -t :sent2vec.git 'cd sent2vec.git; make'`, which gives me the following output:
```
c++ -pthread -std=c++0x -O3 -funroll-loops -c src/args.cc
Assembler messages:
Fatal error: can't create args.o: Read-only file system
make: *** [args.o] Error 1
```

## Reproducing the error for METEOR

I have uploaded the METEOR repo onto CodaLab using `cl upload -g https://github.com/cmu-mtlab/meteor.git`.

I then try to compile it using `cl run -t :meteor.git 'cd meteor.git; ant'`, which gives me the following output:
```
0x16900e65b20049188872a8dad94bc02b
Buildfile: /0x16900e65b20049188872a8dad94bc02b_dependencies/meteor.git/build.xml

init:

BUILD FAILED

issue_title: Allure throws AttributeError when using @pytest.fixture with yield statement
issue_body:
[//]: # (
. Note: for support questions, please use Stackoverflow or Gitter**. 
. This repository's issues are reserved for feature requests and bug reports.
.
. In case of any problems with Allure Jenkins plugin** please use the following repository 
. to create an issue: https://github.com/jenkinsci/allure-plugin/issues
.
. Make sure you have a clear name for your issue. The name should start with a capital 
. letter and no dot is required in the end of the sentence. An example of good issue names:
.
. - The report is broken in IE11
. - Add an ability to disable default plugins
. - Support emoji in test descriptions
)

#### I'm submitting a ... 
  - [x] bug report

#### What is the current behavior?
Running tests with using `@pytest.fixture` with `yield` always throws AttributeError exception


#### Reproduction
Run code code below with argument `--alluredir=allure-results`
```python
import pytest
@pytest.fixture
def fixture_name():
    yield True
def test_error(fixture_name):
    assert 1
```

issue_title: deis/contrib/gce/gce-user-data generates an invalid cloud-config
issue_body:
On `deis 1.11.0`  when running 
```bash
./contrib/gce/create-gce-user-data $(curl -s https://discovery.etcd.io/new)
``` 
the cloud-config file generated is always invalid due to a null discovery value.
This line (in `contrib/coreos/user-data.example`): 
```bash
discovery: #DISCOVERY_URL
````
is always replaced (in the output file `contrib/gce/gce-user-data`) by : 
```python
"discovery": !!null |-
      null
```
After some debugging, I noticed that the placeholder `#DISCOVERY_URL` was already replaced by `null`even before reaching the line (in `contrib/gce/create-gce-user-data`) :
```python
dump = dump.replace('#DISCOVERY_URL', url)
```
So I replaced `#DISCOVERY_URL` by `__DISCOVERY_URL__` in both `contrib/gce/create-gce-user-data` and `contrib/coreos/user-data.example` and it worked.

The problem seems to be coming from this code :
```python
configuration_coreos_template = yaml.safe_load(coreos_template)
```

issue_title: Determine overall approach to long-form vs. short-form options
issue_body:
I have, up until now, intentionally avoided having short-form options for anything except the most essential things (`-h/--help`, `-F/--format`).

I noticed that this rule was broken somewhat in `globus whoami` with `-v/--verbose`, but I didn't mind that much. However, it may have been better to address then, rather than leaving it for now.

As many of Steve's recent items on the CLI pertain to the addition of short-form options, I want us to have a clear decision about when we do or do not allow short-forms which we can apply in all cases, rather than a case-by-case debate about each flag.
@tuecke @ranantha I'm not sure when we could have time to discuss this?

Some precepts, personal beliefs, and pros/cons to serve as a basis for discussion:

1. "Consistency" is not an end-goal or inherent good. It only serves the broader goal of making the CLI learnable and easy to read.
2. Some options are dangerous and should always be spelled out. For example, there should not be a short-form of `globus transfer --no-verify-checksum`
3. Some options are used so often that not having a short form is an impediment to basic usage. For example, not having `-r/--recursive` is awkward and uncomfortable in `aws s3` commands, and I don't want us to have the same restrictions.
4. Some short forms have implied meanings carried from existing CLI tools. If `-r` means "recursive" pretty much everywhere and `-f` means `--force`, we should respect those meanings.
5. Some short forms have _multiple_ implied meanings from existing CLI tools. For example, `-v` is often `--verbose` but also sometimes `--version`. This is a potential snake-pit of confusion.
6. Long forms are always more readable in scripts, and always less convenient in interactive usage.
7. Combined short options are convenient, but totally unreadable. Consider `rsync -rlptgoDA`. Allowing users to produce this kind of string is a bad thing about short options.
8. Too many short options on the same command is inherently confusing, as it becomes increasingly hard to remember which letter means what.

issue_title: What should the structure of commands for managing Shares be?
issue_body:
This is a bit of a design question with fairly broad implications for a bunch of commands.

There's some clunkiness regarding shared endpoint management because they're _sorta_ endpoints and they're _sorta_ their own thing.
That they are valid logical endpoints, good as Task and FS op targets, is good and useful, but only if we strip back the meaning of "endpoint" to be exactly that.
Shared Endpoints don't/can't support the full set of Endpoint operations, which makes managing them as a type of endpoint very weird.

That leads to real weirdness in the API as well -- like all endpoints having a nullable "host_path" field.
At the very least, it would be nice to have endpoint subtypes added, so that we're not deducing the endpoint type from which fields are null.

Ideally, in my mind, non-shared endpoints would be "demoted" to be another specialized type of endpoint, something like "physical endpoints" or "gridftp server endpoints" (which distinguishes them from S3), but that's a bigger issue.
For now, focusing on the CLI.

I started looking at #3 again, and realized that there's actually a bit of a model problem posed here.
Regarding shared endpoint creation in particular, I think we have three options.
  1. `globus transfer endpoint create --shared --host-endpoint-id ...`
  2. `globus transfer endpoint share --host-endpoint-id ...`
  3. `globus transfer share create --host-endpoint-id ...`

If I had to distill down this issue to a TL;DR format, it would probably be a choice between those three options, phrased as
Conceptually, which of the following is a Share?

  1. A Special Type of Endpoint
  2. A Resource Belonging to an Endpoint
  3. A First-Class Entity (which happens to depend on an Endpoint)

How we think of Shared Endpoints / Shares and "what they are" will ultimately drive how we present them and manage them in the CLI and potentially other interfaces.

Right now, the API and Web App share the opinion that a Shared Endpoint is a special kind of endpoint. That works really well when you're looking at endpoints that are shared with you, but very badly when you're trying to manage your own shared endpoints.

Should this be divided into two categories?
I would be most inclined towards:

  - Your own Shared Endpoints are first-class objects which require a host-endpoint as a dependency
  - Shared Endpoints not owned by you (and even your own when found in searches) are a special case of Endpoints

That duality could be confusing if poorly presented, but I think the other split-brained view of the world -- in which managing Shared Endpoints is a confusing special case of managing an endpoint definition -- is more confusing.
Perhaps the Web UI will continue with its current layout and format, but the CLI, SDK, and other developer tools will present things in a different form? I worry that inconsistency will confuse developers by not mapping nicely to the UI, but right now we'll be confusing developers anyway, so it's not exactly a net loss.

I can take whatever kind of decision I want for now, but this needs to be resolved properly before the first "production" version.

issue_body:
We should have a discussion about the development roadmap for tsfresh:

I would propose that we focus on the speed in the near future. 

1. Getting the parallelization right (chunk size?)

3. doing some performance testing with the different feature calculators (see issue #74), maybe speed up some
4. increase number of unit tests (issue #11)

After we have finished that, we should improve the documentation (issues #92, #102, #61, #51 and so on)

Then, when we have finished that we can tackle topics like statefull feature calculators, new feature calculators and so.

issue_title: Logistics for v4 Development
issue_body:
Let's create a plan for developing and releasing version 4.

Proposal: we create a v3 branch for backporting important bugfixes, then implement the planned breaking changes on master for release as v4.

Pull Requests:

 * v4-only changes belong in a PR to master
 * v3 & v4 changes go in a PR to master for review. After green-lit, open another PR to `v3` branch, for insta-merge after tests pass.

Timeline:

 * ~October:
   * Create v3 branch
   * Deprecation warning in v3
   * Start merging in breaking changes to master
 * ~November:
   * Finish breaking change merges to master, then release as v4.0.0-beta
   * Backport any important bugfixes to v3
 * ~December:
   * Normal development progresses on master, delete cruft for backwards-compatible support
   * Release master as v4.0.0-stable
 * ~January: Stop backporting anything but critical security fixes to v3 ([no earlier](https://medium.com/@pipermerriam/dropping-python-2-support-d781e7b48160))
 * ~April: Stop all backporting and support for v3

Prefer to get all breaking changes in before v4-beta, but they _must_ all go in before v4-stable.

To address a couple in-flight projects:

 * steps 1-2 of #294 can go in v3, and step 3 can go in master (v4)
 * #324 is a bit trickier: it would be nice to release in v3, but it depends on an alpha package. Maybe it's best to just go in master, to avoid maintaining a v3-beta branch.

issue_title: Cannot install to non-root env with pip
issue_body:
Running "pip install <pkg>" is consistently installing <pkg> into the root env, instead of my active env. I have reproduced this from a clean (py3.6) install of anaconda:
```
bash Anaconda3-4.4.0-MacOSX-x86_64.sh
```
Next, from a new shell:
```
conda create test
source activate test
pip install speclite
```
These commands appear to work, however the package is not listed when I run:
```
conda list -n test speclite
```
but is listed by:
```
conda list -n root speclite
```
I find the same behavior with every package I have tried so far.

I am hoping this is just user error since I am new to conda envs, but this is not the behavior I expected.

issue_title: Help of miniconda windows installer is missing doc for silent install mode
issue_body:
Hi,

I was using the Miniconda windows installer from http://conda.pydata.org/miniconda.html.

A windows with the following text shows up, when running `Miniconda-3.7.0-Windows-x86.exe /?`:
```
Usage Miniconda-3.7.0-Windows-x86.exe [options]
Options:

/InstallationType=AllUsers [default: JustMe]

/AddToPath=[0|1] [default: 1]

/RegisterPython=[0|1] [default: AllUsers: 1, JustMe: 0]

Examples:
Install for all users, but don't add to PATH env var:
Miniconda-3.7.0-Windows-x86.exe /InstallationType=AllUsers
/AddToPath=0

Install for just me, but register the system Python:
Miniconda-3.7.0-Windows-x86.exe /RegisterPython=1
```

This option list is missing the already! available options
```
/S

/D=C:\Miniconda
```
for silent install mode (unattended install mode) and the installation directory respectively.

Also the documentation on http://conda.pydata.org/miniconda.html, or http://conda.pydata.org/docs/index.html are not mentioning the command line options of the installer for conda / e.g. miniconda.

The only hint I could find was: http://docs.continuum.io/anaconda/install.html#windows-install.

issue_title: Channel priority failed for some packages
issue_body:
Seems `ipykernel`, `ipython_genutils`, `numpy`, and `tk` all come from `defaults` instead of `conda-forge` (higher priority). Now we have `tk` 8.5.19 instead of 8.5.18. So if there is pinning on the minor number this might explain that one. `numpy` uses features, but still I would hope our `numpy` gets higher priority.

However, `ipykernel` and `ipython_genutils` both have the exact same versions in `conda-forge` as they do in `defaults`. In fact, our `ipykernel` has a higher build number (1 instead of 0). `ipython_genutils` is versioned identically. It would be nice to understand why the solver is doing this and see what we can do about it.

issue_title: Feature request - simplify SSH login and SCP
issue_body:
# Situation
Currently, if you want to ssh into a VM; you first need to grab the VMs IP address, and then use your built-in SSH to login to the VM. You also need to manage SSH keys for your VMs yourself.

# **Feature request:** 
Ability to SSH into a VM straight from CLI like:

```
az vm ssh -n myawesomevm
```

Ability to SCP a file into a VM from the CLI like:
```
az vm scp -n myawesomevm --local-file /home/user/file.txt
```
With the ability for az cli to be configurable as to which keys will be used:
```
az configure --defaults private-key='~/.ssh/myprivate.key'
```

# **Constraints/complexity**
I've been thinking about this and reflected on the following complexities:

- **Public/Private IP**: az CLI should be intelligent enough to use public IP if available or revert to private IP if there is no public IP.
- **Key management**: typically ssh keys are stored in the .ssh directory. Az CLI will need to have configurability as to which keys are being used. Potential integration with Key Vault would be great nice to have.
- **Linux/Windows**: SSH is Linux native, typical SSH usage on Windows is using Putty, although OpenSSH has been ported to [Win32](https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH).

issue_title: Explain in docs how to wait for new messages in service bus queue
issue_body:
I'd like to know how I can react to new messages appearing in a service bus queue using the python sdk. As far as I know, I can write `msg = bus_service.receive_queue_message('taskqueue')`, but does that block/poll/something or does it always return immediately? This should be clarified in the docs.

Ideally, I'd like to be able to pass a timeout and wait for that time span to see if a new message appears when there aren't any available immediately. Supporting that feature asynchronously would be even better! Something like:

```python
while True:
    msg = await bus_service.receive_queue_message('taskqueue', wait=500)  # blocks/defers execution for 500 seconds
    if msg:
        process(msg)
```

issue_title: azure.storage.QueueMessage considered harmful
issue_body:
### Description:
This week I wrote code addressing Azure Storage Blobs, Tables and Queues. Great stuff. Since I ran into errors while removing message that were dequeued more often than my limit, I looked into the details of azure.storage.QueueMessage. The implementation was surprising me...

azure.storage.QueueMessage is just a bunch of strings. I was really surprised that this class (obviously nothing more than a Data Transfer Object) ships with an SDK that is marked as 'production/stable/ on PyPI. 


class QueueMessage(WindowsAzureData):

    ''' Queue message class. '''

    def __init__(self):
        self.message_id = u''
        self.insertion_time = u''
        self.expiration_time = u''
        self.pop_receipt = u''
        self.time_next_visible = u''
        self.dequeue_count = u''
        self.message_text = u''

I would expect members having names containing 'time' to have type DateTime and members having names indicating an integer (dequeue_count) to be an Integer. Instances of this class are just prone to error. That's what happened to me.

Focused Python developers may expect a standard-level pythonic API coming from Microsoft that of course will hide transport implementations details as well that are discussed in this issue [https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/150]. 

Can anybody comment this or explain why it is like that or even has to be like that? Any suggestions how to contribute on that important topic without breaking APIs are welcome.

##### Original Issue: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/156
### Comments (0):
